Our Team – Civic Action

# Our Team

Meet the Board of Directors

## Laura Adams

Wealth Management Canada, Morgan Stanley Wealth Canada

Managing Director, Head of Institutional Sales & Trading (MSCL), CEO

View Bio

Close

As a Managing Director for Morgan Stanley, Laura Adams leads the Canadian Institutional Sales & Trading Business and serves as the CEO and UDP of Wealth Management Canada out of Toronto. Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Canada (MSWC) was launched under Laura’s leadership in 2022 and is a full-service wealth management service that complements Morgan Stanley at Work’s stock plan administrative offering, Shareworks. Laura’s career with the Firm began in 2000 when she joined Morgan Stanley’s New York Institutional Equity Division. Three years later, Laura moved to oversee the restructuring of the Institutional Distribution team in Toronto, which included providing global research insights for Institutional clients throughout Canada. Since then, the team has expanded its services to High Touch and Electronic Trading, Equity Derivatives, FX, Prime Brokerage, Credit, and Fixed Income Solutions, and Laura maintains her role as Morgan Stanley’s Senior Relationship Manager for the Firm’s largest Canadian Institutional Asset management clients. Prior to joining Morgan Stanley, Laura held senior leadership roles working on telecommunication corporate strategy in Stockholm and Washington DC. In 1996, she graduated magna cum laude from the University of Georgia with a BBA in International Business & Finance. As an All-American NCAA Division I Swimmer, Laura won an SEC Championship and received the NCAA Woman of the Year award. Laura is an active board member for Morgan Stanley Canada Limited, Morgan Stanley Wealth Canada, and TAS Impact.

## Ronald (Ron) Alepian

TD Bank Group

Chief Corporate Affairs Officer

View Bio

Close

Ronald Alepian is Chief Corporate Affairs Officer for TD Bank Group. He advises the CEO, Senior Executive Team and Board on complex issues, and leads an integrated North American team that strengthens brand and reputation, drives TD’s competitive advantage and market differentiation, nurtures relationships with policymakers and key stakeholders, and mobilizes the energy and talent of TD’s colleagues. He joined TD in August of 2017 as Chief Communications Officer, with responsibility for Corporate & Public Affairs (CAPA) and Canadian Government Relations, and added U.S. Government Relations, Sustainability, Corporate Citizenship and Social Impact to his mandate in early 2025. Throughout his career, Ron has helped some of the world’s leading corporations navigate uncertainty, launch groundbreaking products, build market momentum, and capture new opportunity. He has provided strategic communications, brand, investor relations and government relations counsel to CEOs and Boards in Canada, the United States and globally. Prior to joining TD Ron led the strategy, design and investor practice at one of the market’s premier strategic communications firms. From 2009-2014 he served as Group Head of Marketing and Communications for TMX Group (which owns the Toronto Stock Exchange, Montreal Exchange, Canadian Depository for Securities, and other businesses). He has also served as Chief Communications Officer for CA Technologies in New York City, Vice President of Global Communications for Nortel in Toronto, and Sr. Director of Financial and Corporate Communications for BCE/Bell Canada in Montreal. Ron serves on the Board of Directors of Civic Action and was a member of the Board of Governors of Junior Achievement. As an undergraduate student in early 1990’s, he founded and led a refugee aid and community support program in the newly independent Republic of Armenia. He holds a Bachelor of Arts (Political Science) from Concordia University, a Certificate in Management from McGill University, and completed a marketing executive program at the Kellogg School of Management at Northwestern University.

## Layth Ashoo

Golden Spruce Capital Inc.

Managing Partner

View Bio

Close

Layth Ashoo is currently Managing Partner, Golden Spruce Capital Inc. and was formerly a Partner at Maverix Private Equity where he led deal sourcing and execution for North American growth equity opportunities. Prior to his career in investing, Layth joined McKinsey & Company in where he led and operational and financial restructurings, due diligences, and strategic reviews for clients around the globe. He was previously an associate in the investment banking division of RBC Capital Markets where he was involved in a variety of capital markets and M&A transactions. He started his career as a software engineer at Lehman Brothers. Born in Kuwait, Layth migrated to Canada with his family in 1990. He holds an Honours BASc. degree in Computer Engineering from the University of Waterloo and an MBA from the Richard Ivey School of Business. Layth is a Young Patrons Circle Ambassador with the Royal Ontario Museum. In his time off, you can find him skiing, playing tennis, or reading.

## Nan DasGupta

The Boston Consulting Group, Board Chair of CivicAction

Senior Advisor and Senior Partner Emeritus

View Bio

Close

Nan is a Senior Advisor and Senior Partner Emeritus with BCG. As a former Managing Director and Senior Partner in BCG’s Toronto office, Nan held numerous leadership positions in the firm including Leader of the People and Organization Practice in Canada, People Chair for BCG Canada, and the Leader of the Women@BCG Initiative in North America. Nan has worked extensively on strategy and organizational transformational efforts across Financial Services, Retail, Consumer and Industrial Good industries. She has contributed extensively in the Social Impact domain and is one of the founding leaders of BCG’s Centre for Canada’s Future. Nan is the Chair of CivicAction, and a board director of Leap Pecaut Centre for Social Impact, the Ivey Advisory Board, and Canadian Women and Sports. She has three grown children and leads an active life of sports, fun, and travel with her family. Before joining BCG, Nan worked as a Project Engineer for General Electric, Industrial Motors Business. Nan received her M.B.A. from the University of Western Ontario, Masters of Electrical Engineering from the University of Toronto, and Bachelor Honors Electrical Engineering degree with a minor in Management from McGill University.

Borrowell

Group Head, Co-Head, Canadian Personal & Business Banking

Sharon Haward-Laird is responsible for the strategic growth and leadership of BMO’s Canadian Commercial bank, the bank’s North American Integrated Solutions teams including Treasury & Payments Solutions, Virtual Connect, Retail Payments, and Sustainability, and co-leads BMO’s Canadian Personal & Commercial Banking Group. These teams deliver products and services tailored to a wide variety of customer needs across BMO’s footprint and are integral to the bank’s One Client strategy. She is a member of BMO’s Executive Committee and serves as executive sponsor for sustainability, the BMO Indigenous Advisory Council, the BMO for Women program and digital currency. She also chairs the BMO Climate Institute. Since joining BMO in 2000, Sharon has held senior leadership roles including Deputy General Counsel, Capital Markets; Head of Corporate Communications, Government and Investor Relations; Head, North American Treasury & Payment Solutions; and most recently, served as General Counsel. Michael Katchen is the CEO of Wealthsimple, Canada’s fastest growing online investment manager. Their mission is to make smart investing easy, low-cost, and transparent for everyone. Michael started Wealthsimple in 2013, and has already grown it to 50 employees, with $500M assets under management, while attracting $30M in funding from Power Financial. Before starting Wealthsimple, Michael was a VP at 1000Memories, which was acquired by Ancestry.com. He also worked at McKinsey and Company.

This interview delves into issues of how Wealthsimple grew as a business, and how Michael Katchen built and developed his own skills as a leader during this journey of intense and rapid growth. This is Part 1 of 2. Part 2 will appear next week.

Q: What were you like as a youngster growing up, and what milestones, significant events, or critical learning experiences stick out for you, that helped shape who you are today?

A: I grew up around entrepreneurs. My grandfather was an entrepreneur. He was at one point one of the largest employers in Calgary, where he started a meatpacking business, back when it was a town of 10,000 people. He was an entrepreneur, my father was an entrepreneur, and my elder sister was an entrepreneur. So I grew up around people that loved to start projects and businesses.

There are two milestones that are most significant in how I got to Wealthsimple. One is that I became really interested in the stock market and investing at a young age. I actually won my first stock picking contest at 12 years old. My father entered me in this charity stock picking contest, which sparked a lifelong interest for me, and I started learning about it and have been doing it ever since.

The second milestone was moving to San Francisco, and being part of the tech community out there. When I started my career I was in consulting at McKinsey doing strategy work for big banks and financial institutions. A couple of my former colleagues got into YCombinator in 2010, when nobody knew what YCombinator was. They invited me to move to the Valley and help build a company. We did that for a few years. That gave me the chance to really help understand what it was like to build a technology company and product. I got really immersed in the tech scene in San Francisco.

I pulled those two things together, a new found love for technology, and a long-standing love for investing, into Wealthsimple. And that’s how this all came about.

Tell us about your path to becoming the CEO of Wealthsimple. How did you get to where you are today?

Honestly it’s surreal to think about where the company is today. The business came about very organically. I was living in San Francisco working at this company we were building called 1000Memories, and we sold that company to Ancestry.com.

When we sold that business the colleagues I was working with made a little bit of money for the first time in their lives. They were confronted with the need to do something with it. They all knew that I was an investor, and that I had a system of how I built portfolios, and they all asked me for advice and input.

As a friend, I built them an Excel model that showed them how to build a portfolio of ETFs, so they could manage their own money, and wouldn’t have to hire someone to do it for them. I shared it with my colleagues, and a small subset of friends that have always been asking me for investment advice over the years. And the feedback was “Mike we love this system, thanks for sharing it, but shoot we’re lazy. Could you do it for us? We’d be willing to pay you for it.”

That was a cool experience because I had this group of customers from Day 1. People who wanted something, who were willing to give me feedback, and could shape what the service could look like. I decided to pursue that further. Those became the first customers of Wealthsimple.

I built the first version of the product with those folks in mind, trying to solve their needs and my needs as investors. I think that’s one reason why we’ve been so successful from Day 1, is that we’re very clear about the problem we’re solving and who we’re solving it for. The customers are very much people like us. Young professionals that want to do something smart with their money, but don’t want to spend time on it. That’s the ethos of our product development, and our strategy for marketing.

And the cool thing about that is that all of those first customers, are all now employees at Wealthsimple. They’re part of the team here. Many of them moved to Canada to help build the business even though they’re all Americans from the Valley. It’s been a cool experience working with those guys and girls.

Was the spreadsheet you built for your friends based around ETFs and the practice of rebalancing them? Because I thought that was supposed to be the ‘passive’ way to invest, and yet your friends still wanted you to do it for them.

That’s the crazy thing. The portfolio was simple. You would do a little risk assessment and then it would show you a portfolio of ETFs.

The key to smart investing is discipline. It’s getting on a regular savings pattern, so you’re putting money in regularly, and not making changes to the portfolio.

So the goal was that every month you’d put more savings toward your portfolio, and you’d plug in the amount you’d be investing in the spreadsheet, and it would show you how to invest those funds to rebalance the portfolio.

Once or twice a year you would use this tool to rebalance and make sure that you were back on your target weighting and in the right asset mix for you.

That’s it. It was super simple. And they were still too lazy to do it.

Time to build a company right?

Yeah exactly. The data is very clear on this as well. People that manage their own money are often terrible investors. The sad part is that the professionals that manage money are often terrible investors too.
